full_text:
SYRIANARABREPUBLICEMERGENCY MOBILITY TRACKING & CROSS BORDER MONITORING SITUATION UPDATEROUND 12 | 25MAY 2026
OVERVIEWFollowingtheceasefireannouncedon17April2026inLebanon,therateofdisplacementfromLebanonintotheSyrianArabRepublic(hereafterreferredtoasSyria)hasdeclinedcomparedtotheinitialphaseoftheconflict.Asaresult,approximately33,887individualshavereturnedtoLebanonsincethepeakofdisplacement(153,087arrivalsrecordedon27April2026).Between2Marchand25May,DTMrecorded119,200individualsarrivingacross1,238locationsspanningall14governoratesinSyria.ArrivalswerepredominantlySyrian,withLebanesenationalsaccountingforlessthan1percentofthoserecorded.Asof25May,atotalof366,719movementshadbeenrecordedalongthefourpointsofentry(PoEs)attheSyrian–LebaneseborderwhereDTMtrackspopulationmovementsintoSyria.ThemajorityofmovementsrecordedinvolvedSyriannationals(97%),with3percentLebanesenationals.Anadditional2,211LebanesenationalswerereportedtohavetransitedthroughSyriatoJordan.Duringthereportingperiod,keyinformants(KIs)reportedthatnewarrivalsfromLebanonwerevisitingfortheupcomingEidholiday,takingtheopportunitytoassessthesituationinSyria.Thearrivalofnewfamiliesincreasedthedemandforalreadylimitedhousing,posingchallengesinsecuringsuitableaccommodationforreturneefamilies.Thearrivalofnewindividualsduringthisperiodhasexacerbatedlimitedwatersupplies,healthcare,andotheressentialservices,compoundedbyreportsofdecreasinghumanitarianassistance.KIsfurtherreportedgrowingconcernsrelatedtopersistingsecuritychallengesinSyria,increasinganxietiesamongreturneesandtheirchildren.InIdlebGovernorate,thedeathofthreechildrenandinjuryoffourothersasaresultofalandmineexplosionhasfuelleduncertaintyamongreturneeindividualstravellingtoSyriaseekingarelativelystablesecuritysituation.PairedwiththelossoflivelihoodsandhighlivingcostsinSyria,growingnumbersofarrivalsfromLebanonareconsideringreturnmovements.Thesituationremainsfluid,andDTMcontinuestomonitordevelopments.
This map is for illustration purposes only. Names and boundaries on this map do not imply official endorsement or acceptance by IOM
MAP 1: INDIVIDUALS AT ARRIVAL LOCATIONS RECORDED BETWEEN 02 MARCH AND 25 MAY 2026 
119,200 366,719* INDIVIDUALSAT ARRIVAL LOCATIONSMOVEMENTS AT POINTS OF ENTRY
*ThesefiguresdonotcaptureregularorroutinemobilitybetweenLebanonandtheSyrianArabRepublic,includingdailycommuting,short-termtravel,tradeandcommercialtransportflows,orpre-existingcircularmigrationpatterns.DatacollectedthroughthefourofficialPointsofEntry(PoEs),includingtheJdeidatYabous/Masnaacrossing,arethereforeselectiveandevent-based,focusingonlyonmovementslinkedtothecrisiscontext.Assuch,thefiguresshouldbeinterpretedasindicativeofdisplacementdynamicsandhumanitarianpressure,ratherthanasameasureoftotalcross-bordertrafficoroverallmobilitybetweenthetwocountries.
SYRIANARABREPUBLICEMERGENCY MOBILITY TRACKING & CROSS BORDER MONITORING SITUATION UPDATEROUND 12 | 25MAY 2026
dtm.iom.int/syria
HUMANITARIAN SITUATION AT POINTS OF ENTRY (PoE)DTMflowmonitoringatfourPointsofEntry(PoEs)alongtheSyria–LebanonbordercontinuedtorecordpopulationmovementsreturningintoSyria,excludingindividualsundertakingroutinecross-bordertravelforcommercialorotherregularactivities.By25May,operationalarrangementsdifferedacrossthemonitoredcrossings.TheJisrAl-QamarPoEinHomsGovernorate,whichopenedon4May2026,remainedaccessibleonlytopedestrians.TheprimaryneedsidentifiedatthislocationrelatedtotheshortageofaffordableonwardtransportationinsideSyria,particularlythelimitedavailabilityandhighcostofcarsandbusesforonwardtravelaftercrossing.AtAl-AridaPoE,operatingconditionswerelargelyconsistentwiththepreviousreportingperiod.Movementcontinuedtoberestrictedtopedestrians,whilevehiclecrossingsremainedsuspended.ReportedpriorityneedsincludedtransportationsupporttoTartouscitycentre,aswellascashassistanceandtemporaryshelterorrentalsupportforarrivingpopulations.Meanwhile,bothJdeidatYabous(Masnaa)andJousiehPoEscontinuedtofacilitatecrossingsforpedestriansandvehicles.Acrosstheselocations,themostcommonlyreportedneedsincludedsupporttocovertransportationexpensesandaccesstobasicassistance,particularlyfoodandsafedrinkingwater.Additionalneedsidentifiedincludednon-fooditemssuchasblanketsandmattresses,basicmedicalsupplies,andpersonalhygienematerials.

CHALLENGES FACEDAccordingtomostKIs(64%),newlyarrivedpopulationsweregenerallyarrivinginstableconditionsandwithoutmajorissuesattheirpointsofarrival.Nevertheless,36percentofKIsnotedthatarrivalscontinuedtofacearangeofongoingchallenges.Amongthereportedconcerns,psychosocialdistressemergedasthemostfrequentlycitedissue,identifiedby73percentofKIswhoreportedproblems.Stressandanxietywereprimarilyassociatedwithlimitedlivelihoodopportunitiesandtherisingcostofliving.KIsalsohighlightedincreasinglevelsoffatigueandemotionalstrainlinkedtotheprotractedconflictinLebanonanduncertaintyregardingfutureconditions.Challengesinaccessingessentialserviceswerealsocommonlyreported,citedby52percentofKIs.Inaddition,42percentreferredtoissuesrelatedtomissingordamagedcivildocumentation,includingidentitycards,residencypermits,andfamilyrecords,underscoringtheneedfordocumentationreplacementandadministrativesupport.Asinthepreviousreportinground,theseverityofreportedrisksdifferedbygovernorate.ThehighestproportionofconcernswasrecordedinAl-Hasakeh(100%),followedbyAs-Sweida(79%)andRuralDamascus(61%).

FIGURE 3: PRIMARY SOURCES OF INCOME (% OF ARRIVAL LOCATIONS, MORE THAN ONE ANSWER POSSIBLE)
METHODOLOGYAsof25May2026,IOM’sDisplacementTrackingMatrix(DTM)teamsengaged5,740keyinformants(KIs)across1,238locations(adminlevel4)inall14GovernoratesacrossSyria.TheobjectivewastogatherindicativeestimatesofthenumberofpeopledisplacedresultingfromtherecentescalationintheMiddleEastregion,aswellaskeyinformationrelatedtotheemerginghumanitariansituation.Inparallel,DTMhasestablishedflowmonitoringactivitiesatfourPointsofEntry(PoE)atkeybordercrossingpointsbetweenLebanonandSyriatocapturecrossborderflows(inflowsintoSyriaonly).LIMITATIONSItisimportanttonotethatinformationprovidedthroughkeyinformantinterviewscanbeaffectedbybias,incompleteinformation,orsubjectiveviewswhichmayresultinoverorunderestimationoffigures.Furthermore,datacollectedatFMPsshouldbeunderstoodonlyasindicativeobservations.Theyrepresentonlypartofthetotalflowsandisnotexhaustiveofallcrossingsandroadsthatindividualsmayuse.Furthermore,movementsrecordedatPoEsdonotincluderegularcross-bordertravellerswhofrequentlymovebetweenSyriaandLebanonfortradeandotherroutineactivities.Duetovariationsinpopulationcoverage,sourcesandmethodologyofficialsourcesonmovementsfromLebanonmaydifferfromofficialsourcesonmovementstotheSyrianArabRepublic.
LIVELIHOODS AND INCOMELivelihoodsconditionsforreturneesfromLebanonremainedhighlyconstrained,withlimitedaccesstosustainableincomeopportunitiesandrisinglivingcostscontinuingtoundermineeconomicstability.In55percentofassessedlocations,KIsreportedthatreturneesarrivedinSyriawithoutaccesstoemployment,reflectinglimitedlivelihoodopportunitiesandintensecompetitionforthefewjobsavailableamongbothreturneesandhostcommunities.Althoughincome-generatingactivitieswerereportedin45percentoflocations,thesewereconsistentlydescribedasscarceandinsufficienttomeethouseholdneeds.Dailywagelabourwasthemostcommonlycitedsourceofincome(77%),followedbysalariedwork(42%)andagricultureorlivestock-relatedactivities(40%).Highpricesandweakpurchasingpowercontinuedtostrainhouseholds,whiledependenceonunstabledailylabour,seasonalagriculturalwork,remittances,andfamilysupportnetworksraisedconcernsregardinglong-termsustainability.Furthermore,KIsreportedgrowingdebtlevelsandincreasedfinancialstressaheadofEid-relatedexpenses.KIsreportedthatreturneesfrequentlyreliedondiminishingsavings,remittances,borrowing,orsupportfromrelativestocopewitheconomicpressures,whichareofteninsufficienttocoveressentialexpenses.Morethanhalf(53%)reportedthatincomedidnotmeetbasicneedsatall,while46percentindicatedthatitonlypartiallycoveredthem.Only1percentofKIsassessedcurrentincomelevelsassufficient.Inseveralgovernorates,thesepressurescontributedtocontinuedonwardmovement,withsomereturneesconsideringorundertakingsecondarydisplacementtoLebanoninordertorestoreincomesourcesandsupporttheirfamilies.

related.excerpt: THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 ICRC 0173/002 05.2010 10,000 Mission The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) is an impartial, neutral and independent organization whose exclusively humanitarian mission is to protect the lives and dignity of victims of armed conflict and other situations of violence and to provide them with assistance. The ICRC also endeavours to prevent suffering by promoting and strengthening humanitarian law and universal humanitarian principles. Established in 1863, the ICRC is at the origin of the Geneva Conventions and the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. It directs and coordinates the international activities conducted by the Movement in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. related.title: United Nations Charter
related.summary: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.
bullets: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.

Article 5 / A Member of the United Nations against which preventive or enforcement action has been taken by the Security Council may be suspended from the exercise of the rights and privileges of mem- bership by the General Assembly upon the recom- mendation of the Security Council.

Article 6 A Member of the United Nations which has persistently violated the Principles contained in the present Charter may be expelled from the Organization by the General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council.

Accordingly, our respective Governments, through representatives assembled in the city of San Francisco, who have exhibited their full powers found to be in good and due form, have agreed to the present Charter of the United Nations and do hereby establish an international organization to be known as the United Nations.
CHAPTER I PURPOSES AND PRINCIPLES Article 1 The^Purposes of the United Nations are: 1.
related.excerpt: CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS AND STATUTE OF THE INTERNATIONAL COURT OF JUSTICE SAN FRANCISCO • 1945 CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS WE THE PEOPLES OF THE UNITED NATIONS DETERMINED to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our life- time has brought untold sorrow to mankind, and to reaffirm faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law can be maintained, and to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom, AND FOR THESE ENDS to practice tolerance and live together in peace with one another as good neighbors, and to unite our strength to maintain international peace and security, and to ensure, by the acceptance of principles and the institution of methods, that armed force shall not be used, save in the common interest, and to employ international machinery for the promotion of the economic and social advancement of all peoples, HAVE RESOLVED TO COMBINE OUR EFFORTS TO ACCOMPLISH THESE AIMS

related.title: UN Peacekeeping Capstone Doctrine
related.summary: 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking. During this time, the conduct of United Nations peacekeeping operations has been guided by a largely unwritten body of principles and informed by the experiences of the many thousands of men and women who have served in the more than 60 operations launched since 1948. United Nations Peacekeeping Operations Principles and Guidelines 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking.

related.title: Consolidated Sanctions List
related.summary: Initially convicted on 23 May 2014 by the ICC to 12 years in prison for war crimes and crimes against humanity, the ICC Appeals Chamber reduced his sentence and determined that Katanga’s sentence should be completed on 18 January 2016. Arrested by Congolese authorities in October 2005, acquitted by the Court of Appeal in Kisangani, subsequently transferred to the judicial authorities in Kinshasa on new charges of crimes against humanity, war crimes, murder, aggravated assault and battery.
bullets: Initially convicted on 23 May 2014 by the ICC to 12 years in prison for war crimes and crimes against humanity, the ICC Appeals Chamber reduced his sentence and determined that Katanga’s sentence should be completed on 18 January 2016.

Arrested by Congolese authorities in October 2005, acquitted by the Court of Appeal in Kisangani, subsequently transferred to the judicial authorities in Kinshasa on new charges of crimes against humanity, war crimes, murder, aggravated assault and battery.

As of 25 March 2011, the High Military Court in Kinshasa opened a trial against Kakwavu for war crimes.
In November 2014, convicted by a DRC military court to ten years in prison for rape, murder, and torture.
related.excerpt: 6907993 1 ERIC BADEGE DRC CDi.001 2012-12-31 Male He fled to Rwanda in March 2013 and is still living there as of early 2016. INTERPOL-UN Security Council Special Notice web link:https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Notices/View-UN-Notices-Individuals YES https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Notices/View-UN-Notices-Individuals Democratic Republic of the Congo UN List 2016-10-13 Rwanda as of early 2016 EXACT 1971 6907994 1 FRANK KAKOLELE BWAMBALE DRC CDi.002 2005-11-01 Male Left the CNDP in January 2008. As of June 2011, resides in Kinshasa. Since 2010, Kakolele has been involved in activities apparently on behalf of the DRC government’s Programme de Stabilisation et Reconstruction des Zones Sortant des Conflits Armés (STAREC), including participation in a STAREC mission to Goma and Beni in March 2011. DRC authorities arrested him in December 2013 in Beni, North Kivu Province, for allegedly blocking the DDR process. He left the DRC and lived in Kenya for some time, before being called back by the DRC Government to assist them with the situation in the Territory of Beni.
